data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "No fixed-precision/recall critic can strictly beat self-consistency across a heterogeneous question set; harness strictly wins only when the critic produces question-adaptive confidence scores c(q) such that the critic filters with a per-question threshold that separates high-error questions (where broken majorities concentrate) from low-error questions (where filtering would only cause false flags), concentrating its recall budget on questions where base error is near 1/(k+1).", "mechanism": "A fixed critic applies the same precision/recall to all questions. On a heterogeneous set, low-error questions contribute fragile-correct majorities (false-positive risk) while high-error questions contribute fragile-wrong majorities (true-positive opportunity). A fixed critic cannot distinguish these ex ante (it sees candidates, not the true answer), so its false-positive rate on low-error questions cancels or exceeds its true-positive gains on high-error questions. A critic with question-adaptive confidence (e.g., verbalized uncertainty calibrated against per-question candidate disagreement) can set a high bar for filtering on questions where candidates largely agree (low base error) and a low bar where candidates disagree (high base error), thereby avoiding false flags on robust majorities while aggressively filtering fragile ones. The mechanism is selective abstention: the critic must know WHEN not to filter.", "scope": {"covers": ["heterogeneous question sets with per-question base error varying across the fragility window", "critic with question-adaptive confidence (verbalized or implicit calibration)", "any k >= 3"]}, "excludes": ["homogeneous question sets where all questions share the same base error (fixed-precision critic suffices, per candidate 1)", "critic with no question-adaptive signal (e.g., fixed threshold)"], "forbidden": [{"case": "Fixed-precision critic arm at mean e=0.25, k=5, heterogeneous per-question e in [0.05, 0.45]: harness score <= self-consistency score (net zero or negative from false flags on low-e questions)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Question-adaptive critic arm same distribution: harness score strictly > self-consistency score by at least 3 percentage points", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Question-adaptive critic with random confidence shuffle (decoupled from candidate disagreement): harness score <= self-consistency score, showing the adaptivity must be genuinely informative", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}, {"case": "Fixed-precision critic on homogeneous e=0.25, k=7, p=0.75, r=0.8: harness strictly beats self-consistency (contradicting the claim that fixed critics never help — this is excluded by scope)", "eval": "rubric:std-crit-design"}], "prose_notes": "This is the direct extension of 30e483eb, which already claimed question-adaptive confidence is necessary. The key addition is the causal mechanism: the critic must use candidate disagreement (or equivalent per-question signal) as a proxy for base error to decide whether to filter. The decisive experiment uses a heterogeneous set of ~120 questions drawn from two difficulty tiers (e~0.10 and e~0.35), compares three arms (self-consistency, fixed-threshold harness, adaptive-threshold harness) at k=7. Budget: 120 x 7 x 300 = 252k generation + 120 x 7 x 150 critic = 126k; total ~378k — trim to 100 questions to fit 300k. The adaptive threshold is set by fitting critic confidence to candidate agreement rate on a held-out 20-question calibration split."}
